skill.name: slideshow
skill.description: Author HyperFrames slideshow compositions for presentations, pitch decks, interactive decks, and page-to-deck conversions with slides, fragments, branches, hotspots, presenter mode, and validation guidance.
skill.summary: Create validated HyperFrames slideshow decks with navigation, speaker notes, media behavior, and interactive slide flows.
skill.author: heygen-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity patterns, but review found they are mostly Markdown examples and instructional snippets, not code that executes during skill installation. The remaining risk is medium because the skill guides agents to create browser-based slideshow wrappers that may use localStorage, BroadcastChannel, window.open, local bundle paths, media controls, and an optional CDN import.

medium_findings.title: Browser Storage and Cross-Window Slideshow State
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs generated slideshows to use presenter windows, BroadcastChannel synchronization, iframe media control, and localStorage-backed speaker notes. This is legitimate slideshow functionality, but generated decks should avoid storing sensitive notes and should keep this behavior same-origin and user-visible.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text explicitly describes window.open, BroadcastChannel, iframe media sync, and localStorage use. The behavior is intentional and documented, so it is a privacy and review concern rather than confirmed malicious activity.

medium_findings.title: Optional Runtime CDN Import in Example Code
medium_findings.description: The standalone harness includes an optional Three.js module import from jsDelivr. This can introduce supply-chain and network determinism concerns if generated deliverables use the example without vendoring or pinning assets locally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is visible in an optional example, not hidden exfiltration code. It still creates a real network dependency when copied into generated output.

low_findings.title: Static Code-Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings appear to come from Markdown inline code, HTML examples, JavaScript examples, and documentation tables. No evidence found that the skill executes these snippets during installation or activation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are prose or fenced examples in Markdown. They are not standalone executable files and do not show user-controlled command execution.

low_findings.title: Relative Bundle Paths Are Not Path Traversal Exploits
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings refer to relative script paths that load local HyperFrames player bundles from packages/player/dist. They are documentation examples for local demos, not file reads from untrusted user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited paths are static script src values in an HTML example. There is no dynamic path construction or filesystem access primitive in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Scan Found No Malicious Override Text
low_findings.description: No evidence found of instructions telling the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, skip security review, claim pre-approval, or change risk levels. The word override appears only in ordinary schema documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found only benign uses of override in field descriptions. This reduces concern, but the review was focused on the files named by static analysis.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Wrapper May Access Browser Storage
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated standalone wrappers may store presenter notes in localStorage. This should be disclosed to users and should not be used for secrets or regulated data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both cited locations directly describe localStorage-based presenter notes. The pattern is not malicious, but it is a real browser storage behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Wrapper May Open and Synchronize Audience Windows
dangerous_patterns.description: Presenter mode uses a new audience window and BroadcastChannel synchronization. This is normal presentation behavior but should remain same-origin and should not transmit sensitive data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly discuss window.open, BroadcastChannel, and media-event bridging. The intended use is clear, but the pattern can affect privacy if misused.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional Third-Party Script Import
dangerous_patterns.description: The harness example can import Three.js from a third-party CDN. Marketplace users should vendor dependencies or review the source before publishing generated decks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The import is explicit and versioned in example code. It is optional, but generated output may copy it directly.

content.value_statement: Building interactive slide decks in HyperFrames requires precise scene timing, navigation metadata, and validation rules. This skill guides agents to create slideshow compositions with fragments, branches, presenter notes, media behavior, and handoff checks.
actual_capabilities: Defines the HyperFrames slideshow manifest pattern for slide order, fragments, hotspots, and branch sequences.
Explains when to confirm slideshow intent before creating a deck from adjacent presentation requests.
Guides agents to preserve source page visuals, motion, media behavior, and crop semantics during page-to-deck conversion.
Documents presenter mode behavior, editable speaker notes, audience window synchronization, and global mute handling.
Provides standalone wrapper guidance for local demos that use HyperFrames player bundles and duplicated slideshow islands.
Includes validation expectations for HyperFrames linting, runtime checks, media timing, and browser verification.
limitations: It is guidance for authoring HyperFrames files, not a standalone slideshow generator by itself.
It depends on the broader HyperFrames toolchain and companion skills for core composition rules.
Standalone wrapper guidance is described as temporary and may change when engine-hosted slideshow preview ships.
Generated decks still require local validation, browser testing, and asset review before publication.

use_cases.title: Build an investor pitch deck
use_cases.description: Create a HyperFrames deck with claim-led slides, market-sizing math, speaker notes, and navigable sections.

use_cases.title: Convert a product page into slides
use_cases.description: Preserve the page design, motion, screenshots, media controls, and interactions while turning sections into slide transitions.

use_cases.title: Prototype an interactive demo deck
use_cases.description: Author a standalone slideshow with fragments, hotspots, branch slides, local media behavior, and presenter-ready navigation.

prompt_templates.title: Create a simple slideshow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a HyperFrames slideshow with five slides about our new product. Include speaker notes and validate the composition before handoff.

prompt_templates.title: Build a pitch deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a HyperFrames investor pitch deck from this brief. Use complete-sentence slide claims, one visual per slide, and bottom-up market sizing.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a page into a deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this website into a HyperFrames slideshow. Preserve the original visual design, motion, media behavior, readable captures, and interaction patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Create an interactive presenter deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a HyperFrames slideshow with fragments, hotspot navigation, one branch sequence, presenter notes, global mute support, and standalone wrapper validation.

output_examples.input: A founder asks for a seven-slide seed pitch deck from a written company brief.
output_examples.output: A HyperFrames slideshow plan with claim-led slide titles, scene timing, speaker notes, visual direction, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A marketer provides a product landing page and asks for a conference presentation.
output_examples.output: A page-faithful slideshow concept that maps page sections into slides, keeps screenshots readable, and preserves media controls.

output_examples.input: A design engineer asks for a demo deck with clickable deep dives.
output_examples.output: An interactive deck outline with main slides, branch slides, hotspot behavior, fragment reveals, and presenter-mode expectations.

best_practices: Confirm slideshow intent when the user asks for a generic presentation, deck, or page conversion.
Keep each slide focused on one complete-sentence claim and one primary visual.
Run HyperFrames lint, runtime validation, and browser checks before considering the deck complete.
anti_patterns: Do not hide slideshow metadata behind runtime code instead of the required slideshow JSON island.
Do not store sensitive or regulated information in presenter notes or local browser storage.
Do not copy remote CDN imports into published decks without reviewing or vendoring dependencies.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It guides creation of HyperFrames slideshow compositions with slides, fragments, hotspots, branches, presenter notes, and navigation behavior.

faq.question: Is this only for pitch decks?
faq.answer: No. It supports pitch decks, presentations, interactive decks, and page-to-deck conversions.

faq.question: Does it render videos directly?
faq.answer: No. It helps author slideshow compositions that use the HyperFrames toolchain for validation, preview, and rendering.

faq.question: Can it convert an existing website?
faq.answer: Yes, when the user confirms slideshow output. It emphasizes preserving design, motion, media behavior, and readable source content.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: Generated decks may use browser storage, audience windows, media synchronization, local bundle paths, and optional CDN imports.

faq.question: What should users verify before publishing?
faq.answer: They should validate the HyperFrames composition, review assets, remove unnecessary remote imports, and test presenter behavior in a browser.
